text: The plastic surgery team marks the midline incision, extending around the umbilicus towards the side where the vertical rectus myocutaneous flap will be positioned.
The surgeon marks an extended VRAM flap on the patient's left side, measuring approximately 30 by 8 centimeters, in a roughly sigmoid-shaped pattern.
The surgeon makes a midline incision starting from the cranial marking at the mons pubis and extending down to the linea alba.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection in a suprafacial plane, revealing perforating vessels passing through the fascia of the anterior rectus sheath towards the overlying skin and fat.
The surgeon incises the anterior sheath approximately 1.5 to 2 centimeters lateral to the medial edge of the rectus muscle, revealing underlying tissue structures.
The surgeon preserves the perforating vessels on the flap, taking a cuff of fascia if necessary, while reflecting the rectus muscle laterally to expose the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon secures the medial edge of the rectus muscle to the overlying fat, ensuring protection of the visible perforating vessels passing through the fascia.
The surgeon identifies a prominent umbilical branch of the deep inferior epigastric artery, which passes medially on the undersurface of the rectus muscle near the level of the arcuate line.
The surgeon opens the center of the posterior rectus sheath to enter the abdominal cavity in a paramedian position, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
Within the abdominal cavity, the surgeon mobilizes the left and sigmoid colon, carefully identifying and protecting the left ureter during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery and performs a high division, severing the artery near its origin.
After dividing the inferior mesenteric artery, the surgeon uses a linear stapler to divide the colon at a convenient point. The rectal dissection is then initiated.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectum within the mesorectal plane, circumferentially dissecting to a level above the pelvic floor, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins the perineal dissection by making a wide incision in the perianal skin, addressing the area affected by the presumed malignant fistula.
The surgeon continues dissection through the ischio-rectal fossa bilaterally, aiming to develop a plane just anterior to the tip of the coccyx, with visible tissue separation in the pelvic region.
The surgeon uses a ligature impact device to circumferentially divide the pelvic floor close to its insertion into the bony pelvis after entering the abdomen from below.
The surgeon completes the perineal dissection by dividing the posterior vaginal wall en bloc with the specimen, using a ligature impact to minimize bleeding.

After the tumor removal, the surgeon temporarily closes the abdomen using three silk sutures to secure the fascial layers and staples for the skin, repositioning the tissue planes before fully raising the flap.
The surgeon incises the lateral and superior margins of the flap down to the deep fascia, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon divides the fascia superiorly, then proceeds to divide the rectus muscle at the costal margin. The superior epigastric artery is visibly divided and ligated during this process.
The surgeon dissects the flap in a cranial to caudal direction, carefully dividing the segmental neurovascular pedicles that enter the sheath laterally.
The surgeon identifies the deep inferior epigastric artery pedicle as it enters the rectus muscle from an inferior lateral direction, marking its location for further dissection.

The surgeon skeletonizes the flap to prevent kinking during transposition. The flap is rotated 180 degrees on its long axis, with the cranial tip passed through the pelvis and into the perineum. The extent of the required skin island is marked on the perineum.
The surgeon passes the flap back through the abdomen, removing any excess skin to ensure proper fit and alignment.
The surgeon passes the flap back through the pelvis and positions it without tension on the pedicle, ensuring proper alignment and support.
From the perineal approach, the surgeon sutures the most proximal edge of the skin island on the flap to the vaginal remnant, effectively replacing the posterior wall of the vagina.
The surgeon continues the procedure along the cut edge of the vagina, moving proximally towards the cervix and then down either side until reaching the perineal skin.
The surgeon temporarily secures the flap within the skin defect using staples. Dermal sutures are then applied, followed by nylon sutures to close the skin.
The surgeon closes the abdomen in layers, securing the posterior and anterior sheaths primarily, and forms a colostomy.
